frameworks.name: functional ultrasound imaging (fUS)
frameworks.one_line: neural activity inferred indirectly from cerebral blood volume changes

frameworks.name: hemodynamic response model
frameworks.one_line: behavior timed to slow vascular dynamics rather than spiking timescale

frameworks.name: neural decoding from brain-wide imaging
frameworks.one_line: task variables decoded from fUS signals across multiple cortical/subcortical regions

core_question: Can functional ultrasound imaging chronically record brain-wide hemodynamics in freely moving rodents performing cognitive tasks?
key_assumptions: cerebral blood volume changes index local neural activity
cognitive behavior can be paced to match hemodynamic timescales
tethered freely-moving animals can still perform cognitive tasks reliably
manipulations: chronic implant design and craniotomy approach
probe stabilization strategies for freely moving behavior
task difficulty during a decision-making task
key_findings: stable fUS recordings sustained across multiple months
task variables robustly decoded from fUS signals over time
brain-wide hemodynamic response relates nonlinearly to cognitive variables such as task difficulty
interpretations: fUS is a viable chronic brain-wide imaging modality for cognition in freely moving rodents
hemodynamic coupling to cognition differs from sensory-response regimes previously characterized
limitations_noted: fUS measures neural activity only indirectly via blood volume
behavioral design must respect the slow hemodynamic timescale
